flashcards.back: Non-volatile storage devices that retain data even when power is off. Examples include hard disk drives (HDD) and solid-state drives (SSD). It provides permanent data storage unlike volatile primary memory (RAM).
Total time taken to access data on disk = seek time + rotational latency + transfer time. Seek time is the time to move the read/write head to the correct track.
The time waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head. For a disk rotating at 7200 RPM, average rotational latency is approximately 4.17 ms.
The time required to move the disk arm to position the read/write head over the correct track. It is typically the largest component of disk access time, ranging from 2-10 ms.
Time required to actually read or write data once the correct sector is under the head. Transfer time depends on disk rotation speed and the amount of data to transfer.

The process of preparing a blank disk for use by creating structures like tracks, sectors, and cylinders. It includes low-level formatting (creating sectors) and high-level formatting (creating file system).
Defective sectors on a disk that cannot reliably store data. They are identified during manufacturing or during operation and are marked unusable by the operating system.
A special sector on disk (usually sector 0) that contains the bootstrap loader program. This program initializes the operating system when the computer is powered on.
Describes the physical organization of a disk including platters, tracks, sectors, cylinders (tracks at same radius across all surfaces), and read/write heads.
The set of tracks on all surfaces of a disk that are at the same radial distance from the center. All tracks in a cylinder can be accessed without moving the arm.
A disk organization technique where outer tracks have more sectors than inner tracks. This compensates for slower linear velocity at outer tracks and increases storage capacity.
Using a portion of main memory (RAM) to store frequently accessed disk data. It reduces access time by serving reads/writes from memory instead of slower disk.
An interface between the computer system and the disk drive. It manages disk operations, handles error detection/correction, and communicates with the CPU via I/O ports.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HDD and SSD?
flashcards.back: HDD uses magnetic platters and moving read/write heads (mechanical). SSD uses flash memory with no moving parts (electronic). SSD is faster, more durable, but more expensive per GB.
Striping - data is split across multiple disks without redundancy. Provides high performance but no fault tolerance. If one disk fails, all data is lost.
